content.text: Subword tokenization sits between word-level and character-level schemes: text splits into
reusable pieces smaller than whole words but larger than single characters

. This family — including Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE), Unigram Language Model tokenization, WordPiece, and SentencePiece — solves the open-vocabulary problem that breaks word-level dictionaries. For agent systems, subword granularity determines how prompts, tool JSON, and retrieved chunks compress into tokens, and how reliably rare strings encode without unknown-token failures.
What it is
Subword tokenization learns a fixed vocabulary of frequent fragments from a large training corpus. Common words such as
the
or
running
may map to a single token each, while rare or novel strings such as
unhappiness
or API paths like
/v2/internal/reindex
decompose into two or more known fragments. Unlike word-level tokenizers that emit
[UNK]
for unseen words, subword schemes guarantee full coverage by falling back to smaller pieces or individual bytes.
The major algorithm families differ in how they select vocabulary entries.
BPE
greedily merges the most frequent adjacent pair of symbols across the corpus, bottom-up, until a target vocabulary size is reached.
Unigram LM
starts with a large candidate set and iteratively removes the token whose deletion least hurts a probabilistic language model score over the corpus.
WordPiece
merges pairs that maximize training likelihood (used in BERT).
SentencePiece
is a framework that implements BPE or Unigram on raw Unicode bytes without language-specific pre-tokenization.
Why it matters
Agents consume tokens on every turn — system prompts, tool schemas, RAG passages, and conversation history all pass through a subword tokenizer. Subword choice affects whether
authentication
becomes one token or five, whether multilingual user input tokenizes efficiently, and whether a 128K-context window comfortably fits a long document retrieval pipeline or not.

In multi-model agent pipelines — where an embedding model retrieves and a generative model produces — the two components almost certainly use different subword vocabularies. Token IDs from one model mean nothing to the other. Understanding subword tokenization is the foundation for correctly bridging such components: always pass raw text between stages, never pass token IDs.
Benefits
Open vocabulary
— novel words, typos, and domain jargon decompose into known pieces instead of producing [UNK] failures
Compact sequences
— frequent morphemes and words each get a single token, shrinking context length versus character-level
Cross-lingual coverage
— SentencePiece and byte-level BPE handle diverse scripts without per-language preprocessing rules
Deterministic encoding
— merge tables or score tables are applied at inference time with no randomness
Morphology sharing
— words with common roots (running, runner, run) share token fragments, giving the model implicit morphological structure
Trade-offs
Tokenizer-specific boundaries — the same surface string tokenizes differently across model families and training corpora, making token counts non-portable
Opaque splits — debugging why a string costs an unexpected number of tokens requires inspecting the merge table or running the tokenizer explicitly
Over-segmentation of rare terms — domain jargon absent from the training corpus fragments into many single-character pieces, inflating sequence length and potentially diluting meaning
Vocabulary lock-in — the subword vocabulary is frozen at training; adding entirely new domain terms requires retraining or extending the tokenizer, which requires fine-tuning the model too
Multi-model incompatibility — encoder-retriever and decoder-generator models in the same pipeline have incompatible token ID spaces; sharing IDs produces gibberish
How it works
The subword spectrum

Tokenization strategies form a spectrum from coarse to fine. At the coarse end, word-level tokenizers map each dictionary entry to an ID — sequences are short for in-vocabulary text but fail entirely on out-of-vocabulary words. At the fine end, character or byte-level tokenizers use tiny vocabularies (~256 entries for bytes) but produce sequences 5–10x longer than word tokenizers for typical English. Subword methods target a productive middle ground — typically 32K–128K token vocabularies — balancing sequence length, embedding memory footprint, and universal coverage.
BPE — bottom-up merging

BPE (Byte-Pair Encoding, adapted for NLP by Sennrich et al. 2016) starts with a character-level or byte-level vocabulary and repeatedly finds the most frequent adjacent symbol pair across the entire corpus, merging it into a single new token. Each merge step adds exactly one token to the vocabulary. After
merge steps the vocabulary has
tokens where
is the base character vocabulary size.
The selection criterion at each step is simple frequency: find the pair
that maximises
across the corpus, then replace every occurrence of
with the merged symbol
. The resulting ordered merge table is stored as the tokenizer artifact. At inference, the table is applied greedily from the earliest (highest frequency) merge to the latest.

Byte-level BPE (used in GPT-2, GPT-4, Llama) starts from the 256 UTF-8 byte values rather than Unicode characters. This guarantees that any string can be encoded — even arbitrary binary data — with no unknown tokens. The trade-off is that some Unicode characters expand to multiple bytes before merging begins, so non-ASCII text such as CJK or Arabic initially tokenizes at worse compression ratios than Latin text trained at the same vocabulary size.
Unigram Language Model tokenization

Unigram tokenization (Kudo 2018, used in SentencePiece's unigram mode and some T5 checkpoints) works top-down. A large seed vocabulary is initialised — often all character n-grams up to a certain length. The algorithm then scores each candidate token
with a probability
estimated from the corpus. Segmentation at inference time finds the split
that maximises the sum of log-token-probabilities:

Tokens are then pruned from the seed set by removing the entry whose deletion causes the smallest drop in the total corpus likelihood, subject to a minimum frequency threshold. This continues until the desired vocabulary size is reached. Because segmentation is probabilistic, Unigram tokenizers can sample multiple valid tokenizations during training (useful for data augmentation), whereas BPE always produces one deterministic segmentation.
WordPiece and SentencePiece
WordPiece (Schuster & Nakamura 2012, used in BERT) is merge-based like BPE but selects pairs by maximising
rather than raw frequency. This likelihood ratio rewards merges that co-occur more than chance would predict. WordPiece tokenizers display continuation tokens with a
prefix (e.g.,
["un", "##happy", "##ness"]
SentencePiece is not an algorithm but a
framework
that implements BPE or Unigram directly on raw Unicode bytes — bypassing any language-specific pre-tokenizer. It marks word boundaries with the
character prepended to space-initial tokens, making whitespace handling reversible. Llama, T5, and many multilingual models use SentencePiece.
Architecture

The diagram below shows the two phases of BPE tokenization. During training (top row), the corpus initialises a character-level vocabulary; the algorithm iterates counting adjacent pairs and merging the best until the target vocabulary size is reached. During inference (bottom row), input text is split to characters or bytes, the saved merge table is applied greedily in order, and the result is integer token IDs.
When to use it
Subword tokenization is the default for virtually every production LLM in 2024. The real decision is which subword scheme and vocabulary size suit the deployment context.
General-purpose English agents
— use the tokenizer shipped with the model (GPT-4 uses cl100k_base BPE; Llama 3 uses a 128K tiktoken-compatible vocab); no customisation needed
Multilingual agents
— prefer models trained with SentencePiece or byte-level BPE on diverse multilingual corpora; check that target languages are well-represented in the training distribution to avoid over-segmentation
RAG pipelines with mixed models
— each model uses its own tokenizer independently; pass raw UTF-8 text between the retriever and generator, never share token IDs
Domain-specific fine-tuning

— consider extending the base tokenizer vocabulary with domain terms (medical codes, legal citations, code identifiers) when those strings fragment into many tokens, consuming unnecessary context budget
Token budget tracking
— always call the model's exact tokenizer (not a character-count heuristic) to compute prompt length; different models count the same string differently
How to implement

For most agent developers the tokenizer is used, not trained. The key operations are: encode text to token IDs, decode IDs back to text, count tokens for budget management, and inspect splits for debugging. The following example compares how the same agent-relevant strings tokenize under three common subword schemes.

"""Compare subword tokenization across GPT BPE, BERT WordPiece, and SentencePiece.

Requires: pip install tiktoken transformers sentencepiece
"""
import tiktoken
from transformers import AutoTokenizer


def show_splits(name: str, pieces: list[str], count: int) -> None:
    preview = pieces[:10]
    tail = "..." if count > 10 else ""
    print(f"  {name:<26} {count:3d} tokens  {preview}{tail}")


def compare_tokenizers(text: str) -> None:
    print(f"\nInput: {text!r}")

    # --- GPT-4 style byte-level BPE ---
    enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")
    ids = enc.encode(text)
    pieces = [enc.decode([i]) for i in ids]
    show_splits("GPT-4 BPE (cl100k)", pieces, len(ids))

    # --- BERT WordPiece ---
    wp_tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
    wp_ids = wp_tok.encode(text, add_special_tokens=False)
    wp_pieces = wp_tok.convert_ids_to_tokens(wp_ids)
    show_splits("BERT WordPiece", wp_pieces, len(wp_ids))

    # --- SentencePiece (via T5 tokenizer) ---
    sp_tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("google-t5/t5-small")
    sp_ids = sp_tok.encode(text, add_special_tokens=False)
    sp_pieces = sp_tok.convert_ids_to_tokens(sp_ids)
    show_splits("SentencePiece (T5)", sp_pieces, len(sp_ids))


# Strings commonly seen in agentic workloads
test_strings = [
    "authentication",                        # common English word
    "Refund order #8812 via search_orders",  # tool invocation text
    "sk-proj-abc123xyzDEF456",               # API key fragment
    "naïve résumé parsing",                  # accented characters
    "def calculate_roi(revenue, cost):",      # Python code snippet
]

print("=== Tokenizer Comparison ===")
for s in test_strings:
    compare_tokenizers(s)

Running this shows practically that API keys and camelCase identifiers often fragment into many single-character tokens under older BPE vocabularies, that BERT lowercases input (affecting piece boundaries), and that SentencePiece marks word-initial tokens with
Examples

The following example shows token-aware chunking for a RAG pipeline. Character-count splitting can cut a token in the middle; using the model's own tokenizer guarantees chunks stay within the declared budget.

"""Token-aware text chunking for RAG pipelines.

Requires: pip install tiktoken
"""
import tiktoken


def token_aware_chunk(
    text: str,
    max_tokens: int = 512,
    overlap_tokens: int = 50,
    encoding_name: str = "cl100k_base",
) -> list[str]:
    """Split text into overlapping chunks bounded by token count.

    Unlike character splitting, this respects the model's token budget
    exactly. Overlap preserves cross-boundary context for retrieval.
    """
    enc = tiktoken.get_encoding(encoding_name)
    all_ids = enc.encode(text)
    total = len(all_ids)

    chunks: list[str] = []
    start = 0
    while start < total:
        end = min(start + max_tokens, total)
        chunks.append(enc.decode(all_ids[start:end]))
        if end >= total:
            break
        start = end - overlap_tokens  # slide with overlap

    return chunks


# Synthetic long document
sample = (
    "Subword tokenization divides text into reusable fragments that sit "
    "between individual characters and whole words. " * 40
)

enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")
chunks = token_aware_chunk(sample, max_tokens=100, overlap_tokens=15)

print(f"Source: {len(enc.encode(sample))} tokens total")
print(f"Produced {len(chunks)} chunk(s) at max_tokens=100, overlap=15")
for i, chunk in enumerate(chunks, 1):
    n = len(enc.encode(chunk))
    print(f"  Chunk {i}: {n:3d} tokens  '{chunk[:60]}...'")

# Verify no chunk exceeds the budget
assert all(len(enc.encode(c)) <= 100 for c in chunks), "Budget exceeded!"
Real-world example

A support-desk agent uses a SentencePiece embedding model (E5-large trained with SentencePiece) for semantic retrieval and a GPT-4-class BPE model for response generation. An initial implementation cached integer token IDs from the retriever and forwarded them to the generation API. The output was incoherent: token ID 1042 decodes to
##ing

in BERT-style vocabularies but maps to an entirely different piece in cl100k_base. The fix was simple — store and forward raw UTF-8 strings between pipeline stages; let each model apply its own tokenizer independently.
A second issue emerged during cost estimation. The team approximated token counts using

(a popular rule of thumb for English). But agent prompts containing JSON tool schemas, UUID strings, and camelCase identifiers tokenized at a ratio closer to 1 character per token for the unusual fragments — nearly 4x the estimate. Switching to explicit
tiktoken.encode()
calls for all budget checks immediately brought cost projections within 2% of actual billing.

"""Token budget guard for agent context assembly.

Prevents context overflow by accurately measuring each component before
constructing the final prompt, rather than estimating with character counts.
"""
import tiktoken

ENCODING = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")


def count_tokens(text: str) -> int:
    return len(ENCODING.encode(text))


def assemble_prompt(
    system: str,
    tool_schemas: list[str],
    history: list[dict],
    user_query: str,
    model_context_limit: int = 128_000,
    reserved_for_output: int = 4_096,
) -> str | None:
    """Assemble a prompt that fits within the model's context window.

    Returns None if the required components already exceed the budget.
    Truncates history from the oldest message forward if needed.
    """
    budget = model_context_limit - reserved_for_output

    sys_tokens = count_tokens(system)
    schema_tokens = sum(count_tokens(s) for s in tool_schemas)
    query_tokens = count_tokens(user_query)
    fixed = sys_tokens + schema_tokens + query_tokens

    if fixed > budget:
        return None  # caller must shrink system prompt or tool schemas

    # Fit as much history as possible, newest first
    remaining = budget - fixed
    history_parts: list[str] = []
    for msg in reversed(history):
        text = f"{msg['role']}: {msg['content']}"
        n = count_tokens(text)
        if remaining - n < 0:
            break
        history_parts.insert(0, text)
        remaining -= n

    sections = [system] + tool_schemas + history_parts + [f"user: {user_query}"]
    return "\n\n".join(sections)


# Quick smoke test
prompt = assemble_prompt(
    system="You are a helpful support agent.",
    tool_schemas=['search_orders(order_id: str) -> dict'],
    history=[{"role": "user", "content": "Hello"}, {"role": "assistant", "content": "Hi!"}],
    user_query="Refund order #8812 please.",
)
if prompt:
    print(f"Assembled prompt: {count_tokens(prompt)} tokens")
Common mistakes
Using character-count heuristics for token budgets
— the 'divide by 4' rule fails badly for JSON, UUIDs, code, and non-English text; always use the model's actual tokenizer
Assuming equal token counts across models
— a prompt of 1 000 GPT-4 tokens may be 1 200 tokens for Llama or 850 for a domain-fine-tuned model with an extended vocabulary
Sharing token ID buffers across models
— passing encoded IDs from an embedding model into a generative model produces nonsense because the vocabularies are completely different
Treating BPE as the only subword method
— BERT (WordPiece) and T5/Llama (SentencePiece) behave differently; mixing them without awareness causes subtle pipeline bugs
Reimplementing merge logic manually
— hand-rolled BPE can diverge from the model's training tokenizer on edge cases (whitespace, Unicode normalization); always use the official library
Ignoring vocabulary coverage for domain data
— medical codes, legal citations, or proprietary API names missing from training data fragment into many tokens, ballooning context cost and sometimes hurting model accuracy
Best practices
Profile token splits for high-frequency agent strings (tool names, JSON keys, error codes) with the exact model tokenizer before finalising prompt design
Pass raw UTF-8 text between pipeline components; let each model apply its own tokenizer independently at the boundary
When comparing models, always report tokenizer name and encoding alongside context window limits — '128K context' means different things for different vocabulary sizes
Include representative agent prompts (with tool schemas and JSON fragments) in custom tokenizer training corpora so domain strings receive efficient encodings
Cache token counts for static components (system prompts, tool schemas) at startup; avoid re-encoding them on every request
Performance and scalability

Subword encoding is CPU-cheap relative to model inference but runs on every request, on every text segment. For tiktoken (Rust-backed) encoding throughput is on the order of hundreds of megabytes per second per CPU core; for HuggingFace transformers tokenizers (also Rust-backed via the
tokenizers
library) throughput is similar. Pure-Python implementations (used in older
transformers
'slow' tokenizers) are 10–50x slower. For batch embedding jobs or data pipeline preprocessing, always prefer the fast (Rust) tokenizer and encode the entire batch at once rather than in a loop.

Finer subword granularity — more tokens per word — lengthens sequences and increases attention cost quadratically for standard Transformers. Choosing a vocabulary size of 32K versus 128K affects sequence length: a corpus with many rare domain terms may produce 10–15% shorter sequences with a 128K vocabulary tuned on the domain. However, larger vocabularies require larger embedding matrices, increasing model memory. For most agent workloads this trade-off is predetermined by the chosen model; it becomes relevant only when training a custom tokenizer or fine-tuning from a base checkpoint with vocabulary extension.
Tooling and ecosystem
tiktoken
— OpenAI's Rust-backed Python library for cl100k_base, o200k_base, and other BPE encodings used by GPT models; also used for Llama 3
Hugging Face tokenizers
— Rust library with Python bindings; implements BPE, WordPiece, and Unigram; supports training custom tokenizers; used as the fast backend for transformers AutoTokenizer
sentencepiece
— Google's C++ library with Python bindings; trains and runs SentencePiece BPE or Unigram models; used by T5, Llama 1/2, multilingual models
transformers AutoTokenizer
— auto-selects the correct tokenizer class and config from a model checkpoint; always use this rather than manually constructing a tokenizer class
Tokenizer Playground (Hugging Face Spaces)
— browser-based tool for visually inspecting how different models tokenize arbitrary input; useful for debugging prompt cost
To train a domain-specific BPE tokenizer using the Hugging Face tokenizers library:

"""Train a domain-specific BPE tokenizer with the HuggingFace tokenizers library.

Requires: pip install tokenizers
"""
from tokenizers import Tokenizer, models, pre_tokenizers, trainers, decoders


def train_domain_bpe(
    corpus_files: list[str],
    vocab_size: int = 16_000,
    min_frequency: int = 2,
    output_path: str = "domain-tokenizer.json",
) -> Tokenizer:
    """Train a BPE tokenizer on domain text files and save it."""
    tokenizer = Tokenizer(models.BPE(unk_token="[UNK]"))

    # ByteLevel pre-tokenizer: no language-specific rules, handles any Unicode
    tokenizer.pre_tokenizer = pre_tokenizers.ByteLevel(add_prefix_space=True)
    tokenizer.decoder = decoders.ByteLevel()

    trainer = trainers.BpeTrainer(
        vocab_size=vocab_size,
        min_frequency=min_frequency,
        special_tokens=["[UNK]", "[PAD]", "[BOS]", "[EOS]"],
        show_progress=True,
    )
    tokenizer.train(files=corpus_files, trainer=trainer)
    tokenizer.save(output_path)
    print(f"Saved: {output_path}  vocab_size={tokenizer.get_vocab_size()}")
    return tokenizer


# Usage example (requires actual domain text files)
# tok = train_domain_bpe(
#     corpus_files=["medical-notes.txt", "lab-reports.txt"],
#     vocab_size=16_000,
# )
# enc = tok.encode("Hypertension dx, metformin 500mg BID")
# print(enc.tokens)
# => ['\u0120Hypertension', '\u0120dx', ',', '\u0120metformin', '\u0120500mg', '\u0120BID']

# Load back later
# from tokenizers import Tokenizer
# tok2 = Tokenizer.from_file("domain-tokenizer.json")
# enc2 = tok2.encode("metformin 500mg")
# print(enc2.ids, enc2.tokens)
Comparison with alternatives
All four major subword algorithms solve the open-vocabulary problem but differ in their training objective, segmentation determinism, and ecosystem adoption.
Scheme
Training objective
Segmentation
Typical models
Key trait
BPE
Max pair frequency (bottom-up)
Deterministic
GPT-2/3/4, Llama 2/3, Mistral
Fast; byte-level variant handles all Unicode
Unigram LM
Max corpus likelihood (top-down pruning)
Probabilistic (deterministic at inference)
T5, ALBERT (via SentencePiece)
Multiple valid splits; subword regularisation during training
WordPiece
Max likelihood ratio per merge
Deterministic
BERT, DistilBERT, XLM
## prefix marks continuations; needs language pre-tokenizer
SentencePiece BPE/Unigram
As above, on raw bytes
Deterministic (BPE) or prob (Unigram)
Llama 1, XLM-R, mT5, Gemma
▁ prefix; no external pre-tokenizer needed
Word-level
Frequency threshold
Deterministic
Word2Vec, GloVe
Short sequences in-vocabulary; OOV is [UNK]
Byte / character level
N/A (fixed alphabet)
Deterministic
ByT5, Canine
Tiny vocab; very long sequences; full coverage
Security and reliability

Subword tokenization creates security surfaces that agent developers must understand. Because the tokenizer processes user-supplied text before it reaches the model, adversarial inputs can exploit tokenizer behaviour to manipulate how the model interprets a prompt.
Homoglyph injection
— Unicode look-alike characters (Cyrillic 'а' instead of ASCII 'a') produce different tokens and can slip past keyword filters that operate on raw bytes while still appearing visually identical
Token boundary exploitation
— an attacker who knows the target model's merge table can craft strings that tokenize across injection boundaries, confusing the model about where user content ends and system instructions begin
Context window stuffing
— malicious documents inserted into RAG context can consume disproportionate tokens (e.g., long URL strings with no whitespace) to crowd out legitimate content or overflow the window
Mitigations

— normalise Unicode input (NFC or NFKC) before tokenization; apply per-source token budgets to RAG chunks; validate that user-supplied content does not contain special token strings; use structured output schemas (constrained decoding) to reduce the impact of adversarial text on tool-call parsing
Mental model
Subword tokenization is a
shared dictionary of word fragments

assembled from the training corpus. Common phrases become single entries in this dictionary — shortcuts. Unfamiliar text spells out from smaller known pieces, like composing an unknown word from recognisable syllables. The merge table is the model's private shorthand: it records every abbreviation the model learned during pre-training. Two models speak different shorthands even when they handle the same language.
Key takeaways
Subword methods split text into reusable fragments to handle open vocabulary without unknown tokens — every string is encodable

BPE merges frequent adjacent pairs bottom-up; Unigram LM prunes a large vocabulary top-down by probabilistic language model score; WordPiece uses a likelihood ratio criterion; SentencePiece is a framework implementing BPE or Unigram on raw bytes
Token ID spaces are model-specific — never share integer token IDs between an embedding model and a generative model; always pass raw UTF-8 text across component boundaries
Always measure token counts with the target model's exact tokenizer; character-count heuristics fail for JSON, code, UUIDs, and non-English text
Domain jargon absent from the training corpus fragments into many tokens; consider vocabulary extension or domain-specific tokenizer training for specialised agents
Unicode normalisation and per-source token budgets are the primary defences against tokenizer-level prompt injection and context stuffing attacks
